abstract:
We address the challenge of embedding large-scale knowledge graphs into continuous vector spaces. While the recently proposed TransE method has gained traction due to its efficiency and state-of-the-art performance, it struggles to accurately model complex relational properties such as reflexivity and one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many mappings. While more sophisticated models can capture these nuances, they often do so at the cost of significant computational overhead.

To bridge this gap between model capacity and efficiency, we introduce TransH (Translation on Hyperplanes). This approach represents each relation as a specific hyperplane, where entities are projected before undergoing a translation operation. By allowing entities to take on different representations depending on the relation involved, TransH effectively preserves complex mapping properties while maintaining a model complexity comparable to TransE.

Furthermore, we address the practical issue of incomplete knowledge graphs by refining the training process. We propose a simple yet effective sampling "trick" that utilizes a relation's mapping properties to construct negative examples more intelligently, thereby reducing the prevalence of false negative labels. We evaluate our approach through extensive experiments on link prediction, triplet classification, and fact extraction using benchmark datasets such as WordNet and Freebase. Our results demonstrate that TransH achieves significant improvements in predictive accuracy over TransE while remaining highly scalable for large-scale applications.
